macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Tzelofchad's Daughters and the Bechor's Double Portion

micro_segments.title: The Mishna statement: Three portions for Tzelofchad's daughters
Gemara's challenge: Why does a bechor get a double portion in something not yet in the father's possession?
Rav Yehuda's answer: The double portion is in other movable property, not the Land

macro_segments.title: Rabba's Challenge and the Principle of Eretz Yisrael as Muchzaket

micro_segments.title: Rabba's counter-challenge: The Mishna speaks of portions of land, not movables
Rabba's resolution: Eretz Yisrael is considered already in the possession of those who left Egypt
The Rashbam explains: The verse 'I have given it to you' means already received as inheritance

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon HaShikmoni's Insight: What Moshe Did and Did Not Know

micro_segments.title: Moshe knew daughters inherit without brothers but didn't know if they get a double portion
The parsha was written due to the daughters' merit but would have been written anyway
Parallel: The gatherer of wood and Moshe's uncertainty about the type of execution
The principle: Merit comes through the meritorious, punishment through the guilty

macro_segments.title: Moshe's Specific Uncertainty: Is Eretz Yisrael Considered Muchzaket?

micro_segments.title: The Gemara identifies Moshe's doubt: Whether Eretz Yisrael is considered possessed by those who left Egypt
The verse's ambiguity: Does 'morasha' mean already inherited or passed down to children?
The resolution: Both meanings apply—already possessed as inheritance and passed down to heirs
The irony: They are considered to possess it for the bechor's law but won't actually inherit it
The Song at the Sea prophesied this: 'You will bring them' not 'us'—they won't enter

macro_segments.title: The Daughters' Approach to Moshe and the Dispute About Honor

micro_segments.title: The daughters stood before Moshe, Elazar, the leaders, and the congregation
First question: Did they go sequentially or all at once?
Rabbi Yoshiyah: They went to everyone first, then to Moshe last (verse reverses order)
Abba Chanan: They came to the beit midrash where everyone was gathered together
The dispute: Whether you honor a student in the presence of the master
Resolution: Depends on the master's signal—if he invites recognition of the student, you mention both

macro_segments.title: The Daughters' Wisdom, Learning, and Righteousness

micro_segments.title: The daughters were wise in understanding human dynamics and waited for the right moment
Moshe was teaching about yibum when they approached; they made a clever argument
Their logic: If they're like sons for yibum law, they should be like sons for inheritance
They were skilled explicators (darshaniyot) of the text and law
They stated: 'If our father had a son, we would not speak'—showing legal acumen
The debate: What text were they expounding and how did they know the rules?

macro_segments.title: The Daughters' Righteousness: Marriage and Yocheved Parallel

micro_segments.title: They were righteous and married only appropriate husbands
Even the youngest waited until age 40 to marry—seeking the right match
Challenge: How could they wait past 40 and still have children?
Answer: Because they were righteous, like Yocheved, they had miraculous fertility
Yocheved was 130 years old when she conceived Moshe—born in Egypt but pregnant outside
Explanation: She was called 'bas' (young) because her youth was restored to her
The wedding: Amram made a new wedding for Yocheved with Aharon and Miriam rejoicing
Context: Amram had separated from Yocheved due to Pharaoh's decree; Miriam's prophecy reunited them

topical_tags.term: Eretz Yisrael muchzeket (Land already in possession)
Honor of student vs. teacher (kavod talmid b'makom ha-rav)
